title: Non-departmental public body

infobox.value: (**NDPB**) is a classification applied by the [Cabinet Office](/wiki/Cabinet_Office "Cabinet Office"), [Treasury](/wiki/HM_Treasury "HM Treasury"), the [Scottish Government](/wiki/Scottish_Government "Scottish Government"), and the [Northern Ireland Executive](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Executive "Northern Ireland Executive") to [public sector](/wiki/Public_sector "Public sector") organisations that have a role in the process of national government but are not part of a government department.[[1]](#cite_note-1) NDPBs carry out their work largely independently from ministers and are accountable to the public through [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom"); however, ministers are responsible for the independence, effectiveness, and efficiency of non-departmental public bodies in their portfolio.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

["Classification of public bodies: information and guidance"](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/classification-of-public-bodies-information-and-guidance). *GOV.UK*. p. 13. Retrieved 2021-02-12.

"2". [*Public Bodies a guide for departments: Policy and characteristics*](https://assets.publishing.service.gov.uk/government/uploads/system/uploads/attachment_data/file/690946/Public_Bodies_-_a_guide_for_departments_-_chapter_2.pdf) (PDF). UK Government. 2006. p. 2.

Cabinet Office (2007) *[Public Bodies 2007 (from the UK Government Web Archive)](http://webarchive.nationalarchives.gov.uk/20081211180957/http://www.civilservice.gov.uk/documents/pdf/public_bodies/public_bodies_2007.pdf)*, p. 6

Bradley, A. W. & Ewing, K. D. (2003). *Constitutional and Administrative Law* (13th ed.). London: Longman. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0-582-43807-1](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0-582-43807-1 "Special:BookSources/0-582-43807-1")., pp. 291–292.

["Public bodies: Public body information: NDPB Categories"](http://www.scotland.gov.uk/Topics/Government/public-bodies/about/Categories). [Scottish Government](/wiki/Scottish_Government "Scottish Government"). Retrieved 10 September 2014.

["Public bodies, 2009"](https://web.archive.org/web/20111011170940/http://www.civilservice.gov.uk/wp-content/uploads/2011/09/PublicBodies2009_tcm6-35808.pdf) (PDF). Cabinet Office. Archived from [the original](http://www.civilservice.gov.uk/wp-content/uploads/2011/09/PublicBodies2009_tcm6-35808.pdf) (PDF) on 11 October 2011. Retrieved 10 September 2009.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

UK public sector organisation

In the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), **non-departmental public body** (**NDPB**) is a classification applied by the [Cabinet Office](/wiki/Cabinet_Office "Cabinet Office"), [Treasury](/wiki/HM_Treasury "HM Treasury"), the [Scottish Government](/wiki/Scottish_Government "Scottish Government"), and the [Northern Ireland Executive](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Executive "Northern Ireland Executive") to [public sector](/wiki/Public_sector "Public sector") organisations that have a role in the process of national government but are not part of a government department.[[1]](#cite_note-1) NDPBs carry out their work largely independently from ministers and are accountable to the public through [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom"); however, ministers are responsible for the independence, effectiveness, and efficiency of non-departmental public bodies in their portfolio.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The term includes the four types of NDPB (executive, advisory, tribunal, and independent monitoring boards) but excludes public corporations and public broadcasters ([BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC"), [Channel 4](/wiki/Channel_4 "Channel 4"), and [S4C](/wiki/S4C "S4C")).[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Types of body

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Non-departmental_public_body&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Types of body")]

The UK Government classifies bodies into four main types. The Scottish Government also has a fifth category: [NHS bodies](/wiki/NHS_Scotland "NHS Scotland").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

### Advisory NDPBs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Non-departmental_public_body&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Advisory NDPBs")]

These bodies consist of boards which advise ministers on particular policy areas. They are often supported by a small secretariat from the parent department, and any expenditure is paid for by that department.

### Executive NDPBs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Non-departmental_public_body&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Executive NDPBs")]

These bodies usually deliver a particular public service and are overseen by a board rather than ministers. Appointments are made by ministers following the Code of Practice of the [Commissioner for Public Appointments](/wiki/Commissioner_for_Public_Appointments "Commissioner for Public Appointments"). They employ their own staff and allocate their own budgets.

### Tribunal NDPBs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Non-departmental_public_body&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Tribunal NDPBs")]

Main article: [Tribunals in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Tribunals_in_the_United_Kingdom "Tribunals in the United Kingdom")

These bodies have jurisdiction over an area of the [law](/wiki/Law "Law"). They are coordinated by [His Majesty's Courts and Tribunals Service](/wiki/His_Majesty%27s_Courts_and_Tribunals_Service "His Majesty's Courts and Tribunals Service"), an [executive agency](/wiki/Executive_agency "Executive agency") of the [Ministry of Justice](/wiki/Ministry_of_Justice "Ministry of Justice"), and supervised by the [Administrative Justice and Tribunals Council](/wiki/Administrative_Justice_and_Tribunals_Council "Administrative Justice and Tribunals Council"), itself an NDPB sponsored by the Ministry of Justice.

### Independent monitoring boards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Non-departmental_public_body&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Independent monitoring boards")]

Main article: [Independent monitoring board](/wiki/Independent_monitoring_board "Independent monitoring board")

These bodies were formerly known as "boards of visitors" and are responsible for the state of prisons, their administration, and the treatment of prisoners. The [Home Office](/wiki/Home_Office "Home Office") is responsible for their costs and has to note all expenses.

## Contrast with executive agencies, non-ministerial departments and quangos

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Non-departmental_public_body&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Contrast with executive agencies, non-ministerial departments and quangos")]

NDPB differ from [executive agencies](/wiki/Executive_agency "Executive agency") as they are not created to carry out ministerial orders or policy, instead they are more or less self-determining and enjoy greater independence. They are also not directly part of government like a [non-ministerial government department](/wiki/Non-ministerial_government_department "Non-ministerial government department") being at a remove from both ministers and any elected assembly or parliament. Typically an NDPB would be established under [statute](/wiki/Statute "Statute") and be accountable to [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") rather than to [His Majesty's Government](/wiki/His_Majesty%27s_Government "His Majesty's Government"). This arrangement allows more financial independence since the government is obliged to provide funding to meet statutory obligations.

NDPBs are sometimes referred to as [quangos](/wiki/Quango "Quango"). However, this term originally referred to quasi-NGOs bodies that are, at least ostensibly, [non-government organisations](/wiki/Non-government_organisations "Non-government organisations"), but nonetheless perform governmental functions. The [backronym](/wiki/Backronym "Backronym") "quasi-autonomous national government organization" is used in this usage which is normally pejorative.

## History, numbers and powers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Non-departmental_public_body&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: History, numbers and powers")]

In March 2009 there were nearly 800 public bodies that were sponsored by the UK Government.[[6]](#cite_note-6) This total included 198 executive NDPBs, 410 advisory bodies, 33 tribunals, 21 public corporations, the [Bank of England](/wiki/Bank_of_England "Bank of England"), 2 public broadcasting authorities and 23 NHS bodies. However, the classification is conservative and does not include bodies that are the responsibility of [devolved government](/wiki/Devolved_government "Devolved government"), various lower tier boards (including a considerable number within the NHS), and also other boards operating in the public sector (e.g. school governors and police authorities).

These appointed bodies performed a large variety of tasks, for example [health trusts](/wiki/Health_trust "Health trust"), or the [Welsh Development Agency](/wiki/Welsh_Development_Agency "Welsh Development Agency"), and by 1992 were responsible for some 25% of all government expenditure in the UK. According to the Cabinet Office their total expenditure for the financial year 2005–06 was £167 billion.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

As of March 2020, there were 237 non-departmental public bodies.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Criticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Non-departmental_public_body&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Criticism")]

See also: [2010 UK quango reforms](/wiki/2010_UK_quango_reforms "2010 UK quango reforms")

Critics argued that the system was open to abuse as most NDPBs had their members directly appointed by [government ministers](/wiki/Government_minister "Government minister") without an election or consultation with the people. The [press](/wiki/UK_press "UK press"), critical of what was perceived as the [Conservatives'](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") complacency in power in the 1990s, presented much material interpreted as evidence of questionable government practices.

This concern led to the formation of a [Committee on Standards in Public Life](/wiki/Committee_on_Standards_in_Public_Life "Committee on Standards in Public Life")[[9]](#cite_note-9) (the Nolan Committee) which first reported in 1995 and recommended the creation of a "public appointments commissioner" to make sure that appropriate standards were met in the appointment of members of NDPBs. The Government accepted the recommendation, and the [Office of the Commissioner for Public Appointments](/wiki/Commissioner_for_Public_Appointments "Commissioner for Public Appointments")[[10]](#cite_note-10) was established in November 1995.

While in opposition, the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") promised to reduce the number and power of NDPBs.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) The use of NDPBs continued under the Labour government in office from 1997 to 2010, though the political controversy associated with NDPBs in the mid-1990s for the most part died away.

In 2010 the UK's Conservative-Liberal coalition published a review of NDPBs recommending closure or merger of nearly two hundred bodies, and the transfer of others to the private sector.[[13]](#cite_note-13) This process was colloquially termed the "bonfire of the quangos".[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## Classification in national accounts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Non-departmental_public_body&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Classification in national accounts")]

NDPBs are classified under code S.13112 of the [European System of Accounts](/wiki/European_System_of_Accounts "European System of Accounts") (ESA.95). However, [Statistics UK](/w/index.php?title=Statistics_UK&action=edit&redlink=1 "Statistics UK (page does not exist)") does not break out the detail for these bodies and they are consolidated into General Government (S.1311).[[15]](#cite_note-15)
